translation: I ask you to come with me.
options: indirect command
purpose clause
indirect statement
result clause
explanation: After rogō (I ask), a verb of requesting, ut + subjunctive (veniās) signals an indirect command. The ut clause reports what is requested — compare with purpose clauses where ut + subjunctive shows aim after a motion verb.

translation: I order the soldiers not to flee.
options: indirect command
indirect statement
result clause
purpose clause
explanation: After imperō (I order), nē + subjunctive (fūgerent) signals a negative indirect command — an order not to do something. The dative mīlitibus is the recipient of the order.

translation: He urges us to fight bravely.
options: indirect command
purpose clause
indirect statement
indirect question
explanation: hortātur (he urges/encourages) is a verb of urging; ut + subjunctive (pugnēmus) follows to express the content of the urging. This is the indirect command pattern: verb of requesting/urging + ut + subjunctive.

translation: They persuade the citizens to accept peace.
options: indirect command
result clause
indirect statement
temporal clause
explanation: persuādent (they persuade) is a verb of persuading; cīvibus is dative (persuādeō takes the dative); ut + subjunctive (accipiant) expresses what they are persuaded to do — the classic indirect command structure.

translation: He warns the slaves not to harm the master.
options: indirect command
fearing clause
indirect question
purpose clause
explanation: monet (he warns) is a verb of advising/warning; nē + subjunctive (noceant) expresses a negative indirect command — the content of the warning. Compare fearing clauses where nē means 'that' after fear verbs.

sentence: rogō tē ut ad mē quam celerrimē redeās.
display: rogō tē ut ad mē quam celerrimē redeās.
options: I ask you to return to me as quickly as possible.
I ask whether you have returned to me.
I know you returned to me quickly.
I urge you so that you return quickly.
correct: I ask you to return to me as quickly as possible.
explanation: rogō (I ask) + ut + subjunctive (redeās) is an indirect command. quam celerrimē means 'as quickly as possible'. The tē (you) is the accusative person being requested; the ut clause gives what they are asked to do.

options: He ordered the soldiers to defend the camp.
He ordered the soldiers who were defending the camp.
He believed the soldiers were defending the camp.
He persuaded the soldiers to abandon the camp.
correct: He ordered the soldiers to defend the camp.
explanation: imperāvit (he ordered) + dative mīlitibus + ut + subjunctive (dēfenderent, imperfect — after a past main verb) is an indirect command. The imperfect subjunctive follows the sequence of tenses after a perfect main verb.

sentence: persuāsit amīcīs nē in perīculum sē conicerēnt.
display: persuāsit amīcīs nē in perīculum sē conicerēnt.
options: He persuaded his friends not to throw themselves into danger.
He persuaded his friends to throw themselves into danger.
He asked his friends whether they were in danger.
He knew his friends had thrown themselves into danger.
correct: He persuaded his friends not to throw themselves into danger.
explanation: persuāsit + dative amīcīs + nē + subjunctive (conicerēnt) is a negative indirect command — persuading them not to do something. sē conicere means 'to throw oneself'; the reflexive sē refers back to the friends.

sentence: hortāta est nōs ut dīligenter studērēmus.
display: hortāta est nōs ut dīligenter studērēmus.
options: She urged us to study diligently.
She asked us whether we were studying diligently.
She said we had studied diligently.
She warned us not to study.
correct: She urged us to study diligently.
explanation: hortāta est (she urged — hortōr is deponent, so hortāta est = perfect active) + nōs + ut + subjunctive (studērēmus) is an indirect command. The imperfect subjunctive studērēmus follows sequence of tenses after the perfect main verb.

options: I warn you not to waste time.
I fear that you will waste time.
I ask whether you are wasting time.
I warn you to waste time.
correct: I warn you not to waste time.
explanation: moneō (I warn/advise) + nē + subjunctive (perdātis) is a negative indirect command. perdātis is present subjunctive of perdō (to waste/lose). Compare with fearing clauses: timeō nē would mean 'I fear that' — here the verb is moneō, not a fear verb.
